coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots promoted photonic crystal for optical information storage and sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, An-Quan
Guo, Jiazhuang
Zhu, Liangliang
Chen, Su
coredata.dc:description: Photonic crystals (PCs) with crack-free morphology, high-saturated structural color and sensitivity to circumstances are expanding their applications in painting, sensor, optical coding and information technology. Herein, we incorporated functionalized carbon dots (CDs) onto polystyrene@poly(methyl methacylate-co-acrylic acid) (PS@P(MMA-AA)) colloidal particles via strong covalent bonding. Thanks to the abundant hydrogen bonding groups on the CDs, as-prepared CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film exhibits highly-crystalline morphology, brilliant structural color and fluorescence characteristics. By means of microfluidics technique, CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraballs with angle-independent structural color for optical display and encoding are also demonstrated. Moreover, the magnetism/optics dual-functional Janus and hierarchical molecular-analogue supraballs are fabricated through the triphase microfluidic device and magnetism-induced assembly, bringing about a switchable behavior of PC supraballs in external magnetic field, which are further employed as the pixel points in the panel to store and display the quick response (QR) code information. Ultimately, combined with sensitive hydrogel, the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC hydrogel is not only imparted with an intriguing mechanochromic property, but also showing the rapid response to pH value. The CDs promoted PS@P(MMA-AA) PCs with various attractive optical properties offer a potential strategy of robust PC film/supraball construction for anti-counterfeiting, optical display, sensors and antireflection coatings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photonic crystals
Microfluidics
Information storage
Mechanochromism
pH sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-Fe3O4 NPs and CDs
Synthesis of monodisperse CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles
Preparation of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) supraballs via droplet-based microfluidics
Preparation of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) magnetic/optics dual-functional Janus supraballs and switchable QR code
Preparation of heterogeneous PC clusters by magnetic drive assembly

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21736006 , 21908104 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20190673 ), Fund of State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (ZK201810), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM and (b) TEM images of PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles. (c) SEM and (d, e) TEM and (f) HRTEM images of the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles. Inset: crystal lattices of the CDs.

(a) XRD spectra of the PS@P(MMA-AA) and CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles. FTIR spectra of (b) CDs, (c) PS@P(MMA-AA) and CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles. (d) XPS and (e-g) high-resolution XPS spectra of PS@P(MMA-AA) for (e) O 1s, (f) N 1s and (g) C 1s. (h) XPS and (i-k) high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) for (i) O 1s, (j) N 1s and (k) C 1s.

Optical micrographs of PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film with the structural color of (a) blue, (b) green, (c) yellow and (d) red. Optical micrographs of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film with the structural color of (e) blue, (f) green, (g) yellow and (h) red. (i, j) IR images of characteristic peaks at 3430, 1665 and 1581 cm−1. Scale bar: 200 μm.

(a) Schematic illustration of the construction of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraballs via microfluidics. (b) The optical micrograph of a typical CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraball droplet. Cross-sectional (c) SEM and (d) high-resolution SEM images of the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraball. (e) Optical micrographs of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraballs with different structural color: green, orange and red. Scale bar: 50 μm. (f) Corresponding reflection spectra of as-prepared CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraballs. (g) PL spectra and corresponding fluorescence images of the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraballs (blue: λ = 444 nm, green: λ = 480 nm). (h) Photograph of the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) supraballs pattern of “CPCS”. Scale bar: 500 μm. (i) Photograph of the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) supraballs codes. Scale bar: 200 μm.

(a) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) Janus supraballs via triphase microfluidics. Optical micrographs of (b) red, (c) green and (d) blue Janus supraballs. (e) Assembly pathways of heterogeneous PC clusters formed from Janus supraballs based on magnetic-induced assembly. Optical micrographs of heterogeneous PC clusters, (f) AB2, (g) AB3 and (h) AB4. (i) Schematic representation of the PC QR code prepared by Janus PC supraballs. Optical images of Janus supraballs QR code filled in an ordered hole-array substrate under (j) “OFF” and (k) “ON” states. scale bar: 2 mm.

(a) Schematic illustration of preparation of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC hydrogel. (b) Reflection spectra of mechanochromic PC hydrogel after stretch and compression with different strains. (c) Reflection spectra of PC hydrogel exposed to water with different pH values. (d) Reflection wavelength variations of PC hydrogel in aqueous solutions with pH value of 1, 7 and 14.

(a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles and construction of the robust PC film via bar coating technology. (b) Schematic illustration of preparation of magnetic/optics dual-functional Janus PC supraballs for turnover quick response (QR) code by triphase microfluidics technology. (c) Schematic illustration of preparation of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC hydrogel for stress and pH sensing.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots promoted photonic crystal for optical information storage and sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering and College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering and College of Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering and College of Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing 210009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photonic crystals (PCs) with crack-free morphology, high-saturated structural color and sensitivity to circumstances are expanding their applications in painting, sensor, optical coding and information technology. Herein, we incorporated functionalized carbon dots (CDs) onto polystyrene@poly(methyl methacylate-co-acrylic acid) (PS@P(MMA-AA)) colloidal particles via strong covalent bonding. Thanks to the abundant hydrogen bonding groups on the CDs, as-prepared CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film exhibits highly-crystalline morphology, brilliant structural color and fluorescence characteristics. By means of microfluidics technique, CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraballs with angle-independent structural color for optical display and encoding are also demonstrated. Moreover, the magnetism/optics dual-functional Janus and hierarchical molecular-analogue supraballs are fabricated through the triphase microfluidic device and magnetism-induced assembly, bringing about a switchable behavior of PC supraballs in external magnetic field, which are further employed as the pixel points in the panel to store and display the quick response (QR) code information. Ultimately, combined with sensitive hydrogel, the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC hydrogel is not only imparted with an intriguing mechanochromic property, but also showing the rapid response to pH value. The CDs promoted PS@P(MMA-AA) PCs with various attractive optical properties offer a potential strategy of robust PC film/supraball construction for anti-counterfeiting, optical display, sensors and antireflection coatings.

ce:section.ce:para: Photonic crystals (PCs) have attracted fascinating attention owing to their unique properties of manipulation and restraint of the photons, which thereby shows promising applications in paints [1,2], sensing [3,4], display [5,6], biological coding [7], optical fiber [8], etc. Bottom-up self-assembly from monodisperse colloidal particles, such as polystyrene (PS) [9], polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) [10], silicon dioxide (SiO2) [11], super-paramagnetic Fe3O4 [12,13], is the most convenient approach to construct the periodic arranged PC structure. However, the weak interaction between each colloidal particle would induce the cracking morphology and low assembly efficiency, resulting in the poor PC structural color and severely hindering the practical applications [14-16]. Recently, many efforts have been devoted to solve the cracking issue during the PC film-forming process [17,18]. Song et al. presented a facile strategy for large-scale preparation of crack-free PCs by the synergistic effects of substrate deformation and monomer infiltration/polymerization [15]. Wu et al. reported a stronger assembly driving force (i.e. hydrophobic force) from the long-chain alkyl of t-BA that facilitates self-assembly process and eliminates the cracking of PC film [19]. Besides, the functional nanomaterial, like quantum dots (QDs), is served as coupling agent to reinforce the interaction between colloidal building blocks and impart the PC materials with fluorescence property [20-23]. Nevertheless, the used semiconducting QDs including CdSe/ZnS [24], CdSSe/ZnS [25] and CdTe/ZnS [26], are usually suffered from the toxicity of heavy metals and lack of effective bond force, which motivate us to develop an alternative assembly strategy to fabricate robust PC structure with crack-free morphology and excellent optical properties from low-toxic, environmentally friendly and high-performance materials.

Furthermore, PC supraballs generated by assembly of monodisperse colloidal particles in specific spherical confined space, have a growing attraction owing to their special characteristic of large surface-volume ratio and brilliant angle-independence structural color [27-31]. Microfluidics technology with the advantages of high controllability, rapid mass and heat transfer has been emerged as an efficient assembling method to manufacture diverse PC materials and shape PCs into the on-demand distinct structures [32], including sphere [33], Janus [34], ellipsoids and other heterogeneous structures [35].

In this work, we demonstrated the environmentally friendly carbon dots (CDs)-functionalized monodisperse colloidal particles to construct highly-crystalline film with rare cracks and supraballs for information storage, mechanochromism and pH sensing. At the outset, well-defined core–shell polystyrene@poly(methyl methacylate-co-acrylic acid) (PS@P(MMA-AA)) colloidal particles are synthesized via emulsion copolymerization, whose surface enrich with abundant functional carboxyl groups, providing plenty of binding sites to couple with CDs. Subsequently, ligand-modified CDs are grafted onto the surface of the PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles by covalent bonding between the amino and carboxyl groups to obtain CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) hybrid colloidal particles with CD-functional surface and photoluminescence property. Attributing to the large number of hydrogen bonds from the grafted CDs, the bar-coated PC film from CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles shows an apparent highly-crystalline morphology, multiple structural color and fluorescence emission (Scheme 1 a). Moreover, CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraballs constructed via microfluidic device display angle-independent structural color for the macroscopic optical property of uniform omnidirectional photon band gap. Based on the as-prepared PC supraballs, the PC supraballs array and special “PC” pattern can be displayed on the black substrate, indicating the potential application for information storage and optical encoding. Furthermore, the brilliant CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles incorporated with magnetic carbon-encapsulated Fe3O4 (C-Fe3O4) nanoparticles (NPs) are constructed into the magnetism/optics dual-functional Janus supraballs through the triphase microfluidics strategy, followed by magnetism-induced assembling to multiplex molecular-analogue cluster supraballs under the external magnetic field. Furthermore, the magnetic/optics dual-functional Janus supraballs with switchable behaviors under magnetic field are employed as the pixel points in the panel to display the stored information purposely (Scheme 1b). Eventually, combined with sensitive monomers, it is convenient to obtain robust 3D PC hydrogel which illustrates the sensitive response to stress and pH (Scheme 1c). This work based on CDs functionalized monodisperse colloidal particles could promote the progress in the fields of robust PC film preparation, optical information storage and sensor.

Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw: 30,000), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), methyl methacrylate (MMA), acrylic acid (AA), potassium persulfate (KPS), ethoxylated trimethylolpropane triacrylate (EO3-TMPTA, average Mn = 428) and 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-phenyl-propane-1-one (1173) photoinitiator were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ferrocene, acetone, citric acid, ethylenediamine, acrylamide (AM), ethylenediamine 3-dimethyl(methacry-loyloxyethyl) ammonium propane sulfonate (DMAPS) and N, N'-Methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA) were purchased from Aladdin. H2O2 and Emulsifier OP-10 (Mw = 646.86) were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All above chemicals were used as received. Styrene (St) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and purified by reduced pressure distillation to remove inhibitors before use. Deionized (DI) water with resistance greater than 18 MΩ cm−1 was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-Fe3O4 NPs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: C-Fe3O4 NPs and CDs were synthesized according to previously reported literatures [36,37]. A typical run for C-Fe3O4 NPs synthesis, 0.45 g ferrocene was dissolved in 47.3 g acetone. Next, 1.5 g H2O2 was added into the mixture. The mixture was transferred to an autoclave and hydrothermal treatment was conducted at 200 °C for 24 h. The product was centrifuged for several times to obtain the gray C-Fe3O4 NPs powder. Moreover, CDs were prepared as follows: 0.42 g citric acid and 536 μL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL DI water. Then, the solution was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the as-prepared product presents brown-black transparent solution which was further dialyzed to get blue emissive CDs. The green emissive CDs were prepared under the same reaction conditions by tuning the precursors to 1.051 g citric acid and 0.54 g p-phenylenediamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of monodisperse CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles
ce:section.ce:para: Monodisperse CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles were prepared via seed emulsion polymerization (Fig. S1, SI). First, 0.24 g PVP and 0.5 g NaHCO3 were dissolved in 110 g DI water in a four-necked, round-bottomed flask. After adequate stirring under a nitrogen atmosphere, the mixture was heated at 98 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 5.5 g St was added dropwise, followed by injecting 10 g aqueous solution of 0.038 g KPS using a springe pump with the input rate of 20 mL h−1. The polymerization reaction was conducted for 2 h. Then, 0.3 g MMA, 0.3 g AA and 0.005 g KPS dissolved in 10 g DI water were added into flask for another 4 h reaction at the same temperature. After that, 0.5 mL solution of 0.048 g EDC, 0.024 g NHS and 0.1 mL aqueous mixture of 1.5 mg CDs, 0.005 g KPS were dropwise added in sequence. The reaction was carried out for extra 4 h. After the polymerization, the emulsion product was naturally cooled to room temperature and filtered using commercial nylon net with 200 mesh. Finally, the filtered emulsion was concentrated by centrifuging at 16,000 rpm for 30 min.

The PC film was prepared by a simple bar coating technique. The monodisperse CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal suspension with solid content of 25–35 wt% was dropped onto the paper substrate, which was scratched to form a PC film by a roll-bar with depth of 30 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) supraballs via droplet-based microfluidics
ce:section.ce:para: A microfluidic device consists of concentric silicone tube, 30 G dispensing needle which was designed and fabricated into T-type chip. The as-prepared emulsion (solid content: 30 wt%, flow rate: 0.3 mL h−1) and methyl silicone oil (viscosity: 500 cs, flow rate: 30 mL h−1) were flowed from the needle and thinner silicone tube, respectively, driven by the microfluidic pump. The discontinuous phase of colloidal emulsion was sheared into monodisperse microdroplets by continuous phase of methyl silicone oil. The microdroplets were collected in a beaker. The CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloid particles self-assembled to form CDs-functionalized PC supraballs under the conditions of 50 °C and relative humidity of 80% for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) magnetic/optics dual-functional Janus supraballs and switchable QR code
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) Janus supraballs were prepared by triphase droplet-based microfluidics which contains two discontinuous phases and a continuous phase. The two discontinuous phases are the solution of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) emulsion and EO3-TMPTA dispersion with magnetic nanoparticles (C-Fe3O4), which were introduced into the two parallel needles respectively. One discontinuous phase of C-Fe3O4/EO3-TMPTA mixture includes 0.005 g C-Fe3O4 NPs and 3 mL EO3-TMPTA. The other discontinuous phase involves 20 μL of OP-10 and 0.8 mL CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) emulsion (solid content: 30 wt%). The continuous phase of methyl silicone oil was injected into the outer silicone tube. By tuning the velocity of three phases, the Janus supraballs were collected in a methyl silicone oil bath with 0.1 wt% irgacure 1173 photoinitiator, and then photopolymerized under ultraviolet light for 40 s. Furthermore, the magnetic/optics dual functional Janus supraballs were inserted into a template of highly ordered circular hole array (hole size: 1 mm). Finally, the dual functional Janus supraballs in hole array was filled with methyl silicone oil and sealed by transparent PET membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of heterogeneous PC clusters by magnetic drive assembly
ce:section.ce:para: Before photopolymerization, the hemisphere of Janus supraball was solidified into PC structure after solvent evaporation and the hemisphere of C-Fe3O4/EO3-TMPTA stayed in liquid state, forming a solid–liquid Janus unit. Using solid–liquid Janus unit as building blocks, the larger hierarchical cluster particles were obtained by liquid magnetic hemisphere coalescence with each other under the precise control of externally magnetic field. The clusters with AB2, AB3 and AB4 structure were constructed by two, three and four Janus supraball building blocks merging.

The PC hydrogel was synthesized by close-packed CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) particles embedded in P(MMA-co-AA) hydrogel. In a typical experiment, 0.5 g AM, 0.15 g DMAPS, 0.0025 g MBAA and 0.0075 g irgacure 1173 were dissolved in 5 g highly concentrated CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) latex (51 wt%), which was then filled into a gap built by two parallel glass slides and photopolymerized by ultraviolet light for 40 s. The PC hydrogel was peeled off from glass slides and washed with DI water.

The size, polymer dispersity index (PDI) values and zeta potential of colloidal particles were measured by dynamic light scattering on Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The morphology of colloidal particles was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHI S-4800). The microstructures of the as-prepared CDs/Poly(St-MMA-AA) colloidal particles were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of dried colloidal particle powder was carried out at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from 30 to 600 °C under an N2 atmosphere. X-ray diffraction was processed on a Bruker-AXS D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer to characterize the crystallinity of the samples. An ES-CAIAB250 XPS system was employed to measure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were tested by using an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer. Reflection spectra of PC films were tested on an optical microscope equipped with a fiber optic spectrometer (Ocean Optics, USB 4000). Optical images of photonic films were obtained using an optical microscope (ZEISS, Axio Scope. A1).

The CDs and core–shell PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles are successfully synthesized via a facile solvothermal synthesis and seed emulsion polymerization, respectively, the microstructure and surface morphology of which are investigated by SEM and TEM characterizations. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the SEM image shows the PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles exhibit an average diameter of 232 nm (Fig. S2, SI) with an excellent monodispersity (polydispersity index (PDI) is 0.009). The obvious core–shell structure can be observed from TEM image (Fig. 1b), revealing the darker PS core and P(MMA-AA) shell. After graft of CDs, the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles also maintain an even particle size distribution with a slight diameter increase, i.e. 240 nm (Fig. 1c). It is worth noting that the stack of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles is more compact than that of PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles since the incorporation with CDs. To confirm the successful graft of the CDs, TEM was conducted to show the surface morphology of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles, showing that the CDs are evenly distributed on the surface of the PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles without obvious aggregation (Fig. 1d and e). The HRTEM image of the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles in Fig. 1f illustrates that the average particle size of CDs is about 3.06 nm (Fig. S3, SI). The clear lattice fringes of CDs can be distinguished and show the lattice spacing of 0.205 nm which is ascribed to the (100) plane of crystal structure of graphene (Fig. 1f, inset).

The crystallinity of PS@P(MMA-AA) and CDs/P(ST-MMA-AA) colloidal particles is analyzed by XRD spectra (Fig. 2 a). A broad diffraction peak around 2θ = 21° is presented in the PS@P(MMA-AA) spectrum, suggesting the amorphous structure of the polymer materials [38,39]. Moreover, on account of highly disordered carbon atoms, the CDs also have a broad peak centered at 24°, which is coincided with that of PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles. Therefore, the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles display same broad XRD diffraction peak [40]. The chemical composition of the CDs and CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles is characterized using FT-IR and Raman spectra (Fig. 2b-c and S4, SI). The characteristic absorption peaks at 3500–3100, 1640–1560 and 1350–1000 cm−1 in the spectrum of CDs correspond to N–H stretching vibrations, N–H bending vibration and C-N stretching vibration of amine, respectively, suggesting the amine-rich CDs (Fig. 2b). The FT-IR spectrum of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) (red curve in Fig. 2c) exhibit two new characteristic peaks at 1153 and 1581 cm−1 can be assigned to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-NH-C and the bending vibrations of NH in CDs, which validate the graft of CDs on the PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles. In Raman spectra, the characteristic peaks detected at 1599, 1192 and 997 cm−1 in both samples are attributed to aromatic ring skeletal stretch, C-COOH stretching coupled with in-plane bending, and breathing mode of aromatic ring respectively. A stronger peak at 1599 cm−1 in CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) is due to the effect of crystallite structure of CDs [41]. The chemical states of PS@P(MMA-AA) and CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles are determined via XPS presented in Fig. 2d–k. The wide scan spectra of PS@P(MMA-AA) and CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) show the binding energy peaks at 285, 400 and 532 eV which are attributed to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The nitrogen atomic concentration of PS@P(MMA-AA) is 2.31% which is lower than that of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) (3.21%) due to the abundant amino groups of the CDs (Fig. 2d and h). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s reveals three different types of carbon atoms: graphitic or aliphatic (CC/CC), oxygenated (CO and CO), and nitrous (C-N) (Fig. 2g and k). The O 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three subpeaks of 531.5, 532.5 and 533.9 eV, corresponding to OC, CO, and OH, respectively (Fig. 2e and i). The two peaks at 399.7 and 400.4 eV fitted in N 1s could be assigned to the pyridinic N and Amino N (Fig. 2f and j). As shown in Fig. S5 (SI), the initial decomposition temperature of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles is 373 °C which is 15 °C higher than that of PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles (358 °C), indicating that the introduction of CDs also improves the thermal stability of colloidal particles. In addition, the glass transition temperature of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles is slightly higher than the PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles according to the derivative differential scanning calorimetry (DDSC) (Fig. S6, SI). In addition, to better understand the effect of PS@(MMA-AA) on the fluorescence properties of CDs, the PL emission and time-resolved fluorescence decay were performed (Fig. S7, SI). Compared with pure CDs, the characteristic PL emission wavelength and full width at half maxima (FWHM) of CDs/PS@(MMA-AA) are slightly increased, which is due to the fact of a certain aggregation of CDs on PS@(MMA-AA) colloidal particles [42]. Also, the secondary extinction of PL occurs after the CDs are grafted onto the PS@(MMA-AA) particles, corresponding PL decay value of 11.654 ns, slightly smaller than that of pure CDs (13.60 ns) [43,44].

Using simple bar coating technology, PS@P(MMA-AA) and CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles could be self-assembled on the paper substrate to obtain a well-ordered PC film. The PC films with different structural colors were achieved via different size colloidal particles, i.e., the particle sizes of 195, 231, 255 and 285 nm (Fig. S8, SI) correspond to blue, green, yellow and red structural colors, respectively (Fig. 3 a-h). It is worth mentioning that the obvious large-scale cracks can be observed on PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film that lead to a poor structural color (Fig. 3a-d and S9). In contrast, after graft of CDs, the cracks of PC films are significantly reduced, as shown in Fig. 3e-h. In contrast, after graft of CDs, the cracks of PC films are significantly reduced, as shown in Fig. 3e-h, resulting in an enhanced reflection spectral intensity (Fig. S10, SI). The characteristic peaks of reflectance remain the same wavelength compared with PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film, suggesting that the graft of CDs does not affect the photonic bandgap of PC particles. Meanwhile, the FWHM of the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC films with various structural colors decreases (Table S1, SI), which is attributed to the CDs grafting so as to promote the PC film-forming, reducing the optical scattering and increasing light reflection. Hence the saturation of structural color is remarkably improved [32]. To verify the possible mechanism of crack repair in CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film, IR images of PS@P(MMA-AA) and CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC films were carried out and shown in Fig. 3i and j. Obviously, the intensity of characteristic peaks at 3430, 1665 and 1581 cm−1 in CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film assigned to OH (stretching), CO (stretching) and NH (bending) are highly increased. As a result, the cracks are dramatically suppressed compared with PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film. Those features suggest that the key effect of hydrogen bonding interaction between those functional groups effectively restrains the cracks of PC film, which could enhance the color brilliance and promote the practical applications [19,32]. Moreover, the reflection intensity of the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC film gradually improves with the increasing content of CDs due to the more hydrogen bond existence, benefiting for crack-free PC film formation (Fig. S11, SI).

Other than the crack issue of PC films, the angle-independent structural color is emerged as a significant aspect for PC applications due to their characteristics of wider visibility, non-rainbow effect, and light localization. PC supraballs constructed by a microfluidic device give an isotropic spherical structure that achieve the angle-independent color feature in contrast to the conventional PC film. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the monodisperse CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraballs are prepared via a simple homemade microfluidic device which consists of an inner single microchannel where the discontinuous phase of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) latexes are shearing into microdroplets by outer continuous phase of methyl silicone oil. After the solvent evaporation, PC supraballs with angle-independent structural color were successfully obtained. A typical CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC supraball in methyl-silicone oil displays the white color before solvent evaporation (Fig. 4b). The cross-sectional and surface SEM images of as-constructed PC supraball are shown in Fig. 4c–d, revealing that the monodisperse CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles self-assembled into a close-packed periodic structure in a face-centered cubic stacking manner, generating a bright structural color. The diameter of the supraballs can be manipulated from ~200 to 700 μm by adjusting the flow rate of the discontinuous and continuous phases (Fig. S12, SI). Furthermore, highly uniform supraballs with green, orange and red structural colors are constructed based on the different size of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles, corresponding the reflection wavelength of 533, 575 and 623 nm, as shown in Fig. 4e-f. Thanks to the fluorescence property of CDs (Fig. S13, SI), the supraballs are endued with the stable fluorescence emission under UV light. The CDs with dark-blue (λ = 444 nm) and light-green (λ = 480 nm) emissions are prepared and grafted to PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles engender the accordant fluorescence response for CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) supraballs, extending the potential fluorescence applications of coding and displaying (Fig. 4g). Significantly, the spherical geometry gives a structural isotropy property, thus renders the angle-independent structural color of PC supraballs according to the Bragg diffraction equation. The as-fabricated supraballs with various stable structural colors present a potential for coding [45,46] and displaying [34,47] applications. Typically, the red PC supraballs constituting the pattern of “CPCS” that embedded in the green PC supraball matrix forming a static display (Fig. 4h). Besides, as shown in Fig. 4i, the PC supraballs with three structural colors (i.e. red, orange and green) could be arranged to the specific color-encoded arrays purposely, such as the optical signal units of “red–orange-green–red–orange-green”, “red–orange-orange-green-green-green”, “red-red–orange-orange-green-green”, etc. The sequence with different color arrangement can be imparted with various information for encoding application. Typically, the number of the sequence can be calculated to be 36 based on six supraballs with fixed three kinds of structural color. The combination amount of supraball sequence grows exponentially with the increase of color species and supraball number, which could provide enough signals for encoding, anticounterfeiting and information storage.

Moreover, a triphase microfluidic device consisting of a parallel two-needle microchannel as inner discontinuous phases and a silicone tube as an outer continuous phase was designed to construct Janus supraballs (Fig. 5 a). The two discontinuous phases are CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) latex and the photopolymerizable monomer EO3-TMPTA solution containing superparamagnetic Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. S14, SI), which are broken simultaneously into microdroplets by the continuous phase of methyl silicone oil. Assisted by surfactant OP-10, the interfacial tensions among the triphase fluids are harmonized to form a solid and stable Janus microdroplets, resulting in a brilliant supraball after solvent evaporation and EO3-TMPTA photopolymerization. The smooth surface and distinct boundaries of double-face structure of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) and EO3-TMPTA/Fe3O4 the are clearly observed in SEM images (Fig. S15, SI). The CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles with different diameter could tune the structural color of the PC hemisphere (Fig. 5b-d). The other hemisphere of the Janus supraballs possesses superparamagnetic Fe3O4 NPs, leading to a sensitive magnetic response. Hence, the Janus supraballs could be controlled under external magnetic field. Notably, before photopolymerization of EO3-TMPTA, the hemisphere of Fe3O4 NPs stays in liquid state, retaining the mobility of the liquid, which could collide and anastomose with each other to build a larger molecular-analogue supraballs with multiple solid compartments [35] (Fig. 5e). The structural diversity of molecular-analogue supraballs, including line (AB2), triangle (AB3) and tetrahedron (AB4) (A is the liquid center, B is the solid compartment), are obtained using two, three and four Janus building blocks, as shown in Fig. 5f–h. The solid PC compartments with green structural color symmetrically distribute on the surface of the reddish-brown merged liquid center (EO3-TMPTA/Fe3O4) by the lowest surface energy principle. After photopolymerization of the liquid center, the complex structures can be solidified. More interestingly, the unique photonics and magnetics dual-function of Janus supraballs make them possible in the application of information storage and displaying. A schematic representation in Fig. 5i illustrates the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) Janus supraballs were applied as the pixel unit for QR code display. The Janus PC supraballs were deposited into an ordered hole-array substrate where supraballs can be freely rotated under external magnetic field. The information of “PC” was previously encoded and displayed on the hole-array panel. This QR code can be encrypted by switching the Janus supraballs to “dark” state (Fig. 5j). When the external magnetic field was reversed, the Janus supraballs would be rotated to “light” state and the hidden information can be read (Fig. 5k). Furthermore, the QR codes involving other information can be regulated and shown on the microbead display panel with magnetic response, suggesting an alternative application for display with reduced energy consumption.

Eventually, the 3D PC hydrogel composed of self-assembled colloidal particles and responsive polymers has aroused enormous attention in mechanochromism and optical sensing owing to its excellent flexibility and tunable photonic bandgap properties [48,49]. As shown in Fig. 6 a, based on the repulsion-induced precipitation assembly [50], the sensitive hydrogel of CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC colloidal particles embedded in poly(AM-co-DMAPS) is prepared by direct photopolymerization of mixture of monomers (AM and DMAPS) and CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) latex. As shown in Fig. S16 (SI), the percentage of breaking elongation (PBE) and tensile strength of the CDs/PS@(MMA-AA) PC hydrogel reach to 980% and 1.2 MPa thanks to the chemical crosslinking between the CDs/PS@(MMA-AA) latex and hydrogel monomers (AM and DMAPS), suggesting a favorable mechanical stability. Moreover, the multiple responses are achieved by the PC hydrogel. First, as shown in Fig. 6b, the green structural color of as-prepared PC hydrogel, corresponding the reflection peak of 540 nm, blue-shifts to cyan (490 nm) blue (450 nm) and purple (430 nm) when it is stretched 110%, 115% and 125%, respectively. Similarly, the redshift from green (540 nm) to red (630 nm) occurs by compressing the PC hydrogel to 75% in length. This mechanochromic property is due to the interplanar spacing (d) variety of PC matrix in hydrogel during the stretch and compression (Fig. S17, SI). According to the Bragg diffraction equation, the reflectance wavelength (λ) is proportionate to d, resulting in the structural color response to the external mechanical stimulation. Furthermore, the instantaneous mechanochromic response and reversibility of this PC hydrogel are clearly demonstrated in Movie S1. Besides, the PC hydrogel also possesses the pH-sensitive property. Fig. 6c displays the reflection spectra of the PC hydrogel exposed to water with different pH values. As the pH value increases from 1 to 14, the reflection peak shifts from 478 to 590 nm, which is attributed to that the enriched carboxyl groups on composite spheres are dissociated to carboxylate ions with increasing pH values, thus expanding the hydrogel volume for the stronger electrostatic repulsion among spheres. It's worth noting that the repeatability of the PC hydrogel is investigated by alternately immersed in the aqueous solutions with pH value of 1, 7 and 14 for 6 cycles (Fig. 6d), clearly indicating that the changes of the peak positions are reversible, which is of great significance for practical applications.

In summary, we have successfully demonstrated an effective strategy to construct PC films and supraballs with tunable high-saturation structural color, modified by the photoluminescent, heavy metal-free and environmentally friendly CDs. The as-prepared CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) colloidal particles are facilely assembled into the highly-crystalline PC film with close-packed periodic arranged structure via hydrogen bonding interaction that enriched in grafted CDs, enhancing the structural color brilliance. Moreover, fabricated by simply microfluidics technology, uniform spherical PC supraballs can be obtained, which not only possess stable strong fluorescence attribute, but also show brighter angle-independent structural color. In addition, dual-functional Janus supraballs with superparamagnetic and optical properties are prepared using the triphase microfluidic device, which could be subsequently assembled into various hierarchical PC clusters driven by magnetism. Take advantage of tunable photonic bandgap of the PCs supraballs, multiplex optic signals were introduced for a favorable encoding and switchable QR code display. Eventually, combining with sensitive polymers, the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) PC hydrogel was developed to exhibit obvious mechanochromism and pH sensing properties. It is reasonable to envision that the CDs/PS@P(MMA-AA) hybrid colloidal particles are not only satisfied for elimination of film deposition defects, but also worked as various functional supraballs for coding and information communication, as well as sensing materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21736006, 21908104), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190673), Fund of State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (ZK201810), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
